story:
No one remembers the day the world shattered. They say it happened in a blink—too bright to witness, too fast to accept—but we all remember the result. It happened, and nothing has been the same. Four hundred years later we cling to a fractured float of rubble, more cloud than land, the white wall forever eating away at the edges. As a kid I skyhopped the cloud-bridges with Reese and Van to greet the Scouts returning from their Skywanders. Those memories of home are a dream, but the days we live now are a nightmares.

I’m a lookout at The Rim, half a bottle of Ironeye Whiskey into my shift, watching the sun paint the White Rush purple and orange while I count the late Scouts. Fewer and fewer come back. No alarms anymore; return is a privilege now. My mother begs me to leave this post, to work far from the White Rush, but that’s for Earthblood at The Spire. They don’t see us as equals. I’m not even a Scout. I just watch. Still, she almost sold her Cloudforge to float me long enough to quit. Crazy. Forging is all she has. Seeking is all I’m worth. The Rim is where we belong. Vaults run express exams every other week to make more Scouts, and even then there aren’t enough relics coming home.

Reese can’t stop talking about signing up. He wants adventure; I want The Distants—somewhere safe, away from The Rim and out from under The Spire’s thumb. He climbs up to my outpost with a grin and a purple card. A Provisional Wander License, courtesy of Van’s pull at The Spire. His ginger hair flops over an uneven tan from too many lookouts at Bright Point—the sun’s harsher there because of The Scar. I tell him Scouts aren’t paid like they used to be. He laughs. It’s not about coin, he says. It’s about wonder. Freedom. Freedom from fear. From the men at The Spire, even if it lasts only a breath.

He isn’t wrong. The Spire feels like it flips a coin when it decides the lives of people it never sees. Without relics there’s no Tether Tax, and without tax the High Harrow orders Skyyards cut loose into the White Rush. Maybe we do need more Scouts to keep our homes from slipping into the white. But it won’t be me. My father never came back from the Rush. That was the day I stopped wanting to Skywander. My mother keeps his Slipskin in the basement like a shrine, but to me it’s a wound. I never opened the letter he left. I won’t open her grief again.

Reese begs me to join. Vicious and Shaw did it, he says. We worshipped them once, the legends who hauled iridescent relics taller than we were. They’re dead now. The Rush got them too. I tell Reese no. He knows why.

Then Micah, a Chamber Chaser, scrambles up the ladder, breathless, eyes wide. He says he’s sorry before the words come out. Reese’s Skyyard is going to be released.

The White Rush hums louder, like a laugh. The world shattered once. Mine is about to shatter again.
